required.title: [Read] Destiny Disrupted: A History of the World Through Islamic Eyes
required.author: Tamim Ansary

suggested.title: [Read] Muhammad: His Life Based on the Earliest Sources
suggested.author: Martin Lings

competence_markers.description: The student can explain tawhid and its implications for Islamic life and thought
The student can describe the Quran's self-understanding and how it differs from the Bible's
The student can narrate Muhammad's life and identify his multiple roles
The student can explain the Five Pillars and what each one produces
The student can describe the Islamic golden age with specific intellectual contributions
The student can explain the Sunni-Shia split with historical context
The student can distinguish between jihad in classical jurisprudence and contemporary jihadism
The student can present the honest record — slave trade, dhimmi system, Islamism — with context
The student can map Islam as a worldview using the six diagnostic questions

narration_prompts.prompt: What is tawhid, and why is it the foundation of everything in Islam?
narration_prompts.detail: 2. **How does the Quran differ from the Bible in what it claims about itself?**
What was his message? Why was it threatening to Mecca's leaders?

narration_prompts.prompt: Explain the Five Pillars and what each one produces
narration_prompts.detail: — not just what it is, but what it does.

narration_prompts.prompt: What did Islamic civilization contribute to world knowledge?
narration_prompts.detail: Why does this matter for understanding Islam?
What caused it? What are the consequences?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the difference between Islam and Islamism?

cross_connections.description: The Islamic golden age, the Crusades, and the Ottoman Empire.
Islam's relationship to its Abrahamic predecessors — the shared theology and the points of explicit disagreement.
Zoroastrian influence on the concepts Islam inherited through Judaism and Christianity.
Sharia as a comprehensive legal and political framework.

content.why_this_matters:
Islam is the world's second-largest religion, with nearly two billion adherents. It is also the most misunderstood major religion in the contemporary West — distorted in both directions. One distortion reduces Islam to terrorism and oppression. The other distortion presents it as nothing but peace and tolerance. Neither is accurate. Islam is a vast, sophisticated tradition that produced one of history's greatest civilizations, that contains profound theological and philosophical insights, and that — like every tradition in this curriculum — has a record that includes both extraordinary achievements and genuine failures.

The word "Islam" means "submission" — submission to the will of God. This is not submission as weakness. It is submission as alignment with the deepest reality of the universe. A Muslim is "one who submits." The claim is that submission to God is the natural and proper state of the human being — that the entire universe submits to God's will (the sun rises, gravity holds, the seasons turn), and that humans alone have the freedom to resist, and the tragedy to do so.

This packet presents Islam from the inside — as its most thoughtful practitioners understand it — before engaging in any critique.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Islamic theology presented from within** — tawhid, the Quran, Muhammad, the Five Pillars
2. **Islamic civilization** — the golden age, the legal tradition, the artistic tradition
3. **The Sunni-Shia split** — its origins and consequences
4. **Jihad — honest treatment** — both meanings, the jurisprudential tradition, the modern distortions
5. **The honest record** — the slave trade, the dhimmi system, contemporary Islamism
6. **Islam mapped as a worldview** using the six diagnostic questions
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Tawhid — The Absolute Oneness of God

The foundation of everything in Islam is tawhid: the absolute, uncompromising oneness of God. "Say: He is God, the One. God, the Eternal Refuge. He neither begets nor is begotten, nor is there to Him any equivalent" (Quran 112:1–4).

There is nothing like God. God has no partners, no sons, no divisions. The worst sin in Islam — the one unforgivable sin — is shirk: associating partners with God. This is why Islam rejects the Christian Trinity: the claim that God has a Son, that Jesus is God incarnate, is shirk. This is not a minor theological disagreement. It is, from the Islamic perspective, the most fundamental error a human being can make.

Tawhid is not merely a theological claim. It is the organizing principle of all of life. If God is absolutely one, then submission to God is the organizing principle of all human activity — law, politics, economics, family life, art. The Western concept of separating religion from public life is, from a traditional Islamic perspective, incoherent. If God is the lord of everything, there is no domain of life from which God can be excluded.

### Part 2: The Quran — The Direct Word of God

The Quran occupies a different position in Islam than the Bible occupies in Christianity. Christians believe the Bible was inspired by God but written by human authors — with their own styles, perspectives, and limitations. Muslims believe the Quran is the literal word of God — not inspired by God but dictated by God through the angel Gabriel to Muhammad over twenty-three years. The Arabic text is not merely a human book about God. It is God's speech in human language.

This has consequences. The Quran in Arabic is considered untranslatable in the fullest sense — translations are interpretations, not the Quran itself. The recitation of the Quran in Arabic is itself a sacred act. The beauty of the Arabic text is considered a miracle — proof of its divine origin. Islamic calligraphy — the art of writing the Quran beautifully — is one of the world's great artistic traditions, and it exists because in Islam, the beauty of God's word is expressed through writing, not through images.

### Part 3: Muhammad — The Seal of the Prophets

Muhammad ibn Abdullah was born in Mecca around 570 CE. Orphaned young, raised by his uncle, he became a merchant known for his honesty — al-Amin, "the trustworthy." In 610 CE, while meditating in a cave on Mount Hira, the angel Gabriel appeared and commanded: "Read!" (Iqra!). Muhammad was terrified. He could not read. But the revelation came, and it continued for twenty-three years until his death in 632 CE.

Muhammad's message was radical in its context: the idols of Mecca were false; there was only one God; the wealthy had obligations to the poor; the tribal system of Arabia must give way to a community of faith (umma) that transcended tribe, race, and nationality. Mecca's ruling elite saw this as a direct threat to their power and revenue (Mecca was a pilgrimage center for the idol-worshipping tribes). Muhammad and his followers were persecuted, and in 622 CE they emigrated to Medina — the Hijra — which marks year one of the Islamic calendar.

In Medina, Muhammad was not only a prophet but a statesman, a judge, a military commander, and the organizer of a new kind of community. The fusion of religious and political authority in Muhammad's career is not an accident or an aberration — it is foundational. Islam does not naturally distinguish between the religious and the political in the way Western Christianity learned to do.

Muslims believe Muhammad is the Seal of the Prophets — the last in a line that includes Adam, Abraham, Moses, David, and Jesus. All were prophets of the same God. All delivered God's message. But previous revelations were corrupted or lost over time. The Quran is the final, complete, uncorrupted revelation.

### Part 4: The Five Pillars — The Structure of Muslim Life

1. **Shahada** — "There is no god but God, and Muhammad is his messenger." The declaration of faith. To say it sincerely, before witnesses, is to become a Muslim.

2. **Salat** — Five daily prayers, performed at dawn, noon, mid-afternoon, sunset, and night, facing Mecca. The call to prayer (adhan) sounds from the minaret. The prayers involve specific physical postures — standing, bowing, prostrating — that embody submission physically.

3. **Zakat** — Obligatory charitable giving — typically 2.5% of one's wealth annually. This is not charity in the Western sense of voluntary generosity. It is an obligation — a right that the poor hold on the wealth of the rich. Economic justice is built into the structure of Muslim life.

4. **Sawm** — Fasting during Ramadan, the ninth month of the Islamic calendar. From dawn to sunset, Muslims abstain from food, drink, and sexual relations. The fast builds discipline, solidarity with the hungry, and awareness of dependence on God.

5. **Hajj** — The pilgrimage to Mecca, required at least once in a lifetime for every Muslim who is physically and financially able. During Hajj, all pilgrims wear simple white garments — erasing distinctions of wealth and status. The Hajj is one of the largest annual gatherings of human beings on Earth — millions of people, from every country, every race, every economic station, performing the same rituals in the same place.

### Part 5: Islamic Law — Sharia

Sharia means "the path to the watering hole" — the way to sustenance, to right living, to God. It is not merely a legal code. It is a comprehensive framework for how to live a life pleasing to God, covering worship, family law, commercial law, criminal law, and governance.

Sharia is derived from four sources: the Quran, the Hadith (recorded sayings and actions of Muhammad), ijma (consensus of scholars), and qiyas (analogical reasoning). The development of Islamic jurisprudence (fiqh) over fourteen centuries is one of the most sustained intellectual projects in human history, comparable to the Talmudic tradition in Judaism.

There are multiple schools of Islamic jurisprudence (Hanafi, Maliki, Shafi'i, Hanbali in Sunni Islam; Ja'fari in Shia Islam) — each with somewhat different interpretations. Islam is not a monolith. The range of legal opinion within the tradition is vast.

### Part 6: The Islamic Golden Age

From roughly the 8th to the 13th centuries, Islamic civilization was the most intellectually advanced on Earth.

Baghdad, under the Abbasid caliphate, was the intellectual center of the world. The House of Wisdom (Bayt al-Hikma) was a library, translation center, and academy where scholars translated Greek, Persian, and Indian texts into Arabic, preserved them, and extended them.

**What Islamic scholars contributed:**
- **Algebra** (al-jabr) — the mathematical discipline, invented by al-Khwarizmi
- **The numeral system** — "Arabic numerals" that the world uses today (originally Indian, transmitted through Islamic scholars)
- **Optics** — Ibn al-Haytham (Alhazen) is considered the father of modern optics
- **Medicine** — Ibn Sina (Avicenna) wrote the Canon of Medicine, used as a medical textbook in Europe for centuries
- **Philosophy** — Ibn Rushd (Averroes) preserved and extended Aristotelian philosophy; his commentaries were studied throughout medieval Europe
- **Architecture** — the Alhambra, the Dome of the Rock, the great mosques of Istanbul, Cairo, and Isfahan

Much of what medieval Europe knew of Greek philosophy arrived through Arabic translations. The chain from ancient Athens to modern Western thought runs through Baghdad.

### Part 7: The Sunni-Shia Split

Muhammad died in 632 CE without clearly designating a successor. The resulting dispute split Islam permanently.

**Sunni Islam** (approximately 85–90% of Muslims): The community chose Abu Bakr, Muhammad's father-in-law and close companion, as the first caliph (successor). The caliphate passed through a series of elected or chosen leaders.

**Shia Islam** (approximately 10–15%): Ali ibn Abi Talib, Muhammad's cousin and son-in-law, was the rightful successor — and leadership should have passed through Muhammad's family line. Ali eventually became the fourth caliph but was assassinated. His son Husayn was killed at the Battle of Karbala (680 CE) — a martyrdom that is the central event in Shia spiritual consciousness.

The split is not merely political. It has produced different theological emphases, different legal traditions, different devotional practices, and 1,400 years of intermittent conflict. Understanding the Sunni-Shia divide is essential for understanding contemporary Middle Eastern politics.

### Part 8: Jihad — The Honest Treatment

The Arabic word jihad means "struggle" or "striving." It has two established meanings:

**The greater jihad** (al-jihad al-akbar): The internal spiritual struggle to submit to God — to overcome selfishness, greed, anger, and the lower impulses of the self. This is the primary meaning in classical Islamic tradition.

**The lesser jihad** (al-jihad al-asghar): Armed struggle in defense of the faith or the Muslim community. Classical Islamic jurisprudence developed detailed rules: jihad can only be declared by legitimate authority; civilians, women, children, the elderly, and religious leaders cannot be targeted; trees and crops cannot be destroyed; prisoners must be treated humanely. These rules were, like the rules of war in every tradition, frequently violated in practice.

Contemporary jihadist movements — al-Qaeda, ISIS — represent a specific modern political ideology (Islamism) that selectively reads the tradition to justify violence that classical jurisprudence prohibits. The student must learn to distinguish between the 1,400-year tradition and its contemporary political distortions — just as they must distinguish between Christianity and the Ku Klux Klan, or between Buddhism and Buddhist nationalism in Myanmar.

### Part 9: The Honest Record

**The Islamic slave trade:** Islam did not abolish slavery. The Islamic slave trade predated the Atlantic slave trade by centuries and continued in some areas into the 20th century. It encompassed East Africa, Central Asia, and Southern Europe. The Quran contains provisions for the humane treatment of enslaved people and encourages manumission — but it does not prohibit slavery. This is a tension within the tradition that must be acknowledged.

**The dhimmi system:** Non-Muslims (primarily Christians and Jews) in Islamic states were classified as dhimmi — "protected peoples." They could practice their religion, maintain their communities, and access legal protections, but they paid a special tax (jizya), faced restrictions on building houses of worship, and were socially subordinate. The system ranged from relatively tolerant (the early Abbasid period, the Ottoman millet system) to genuinely oppressive, depending on the ruler and the era. It was better than what most religious minorities experienced in medieval Europe — but it was not equality.

**Contemporary Islamism:** The 20th-century movements — the Muslim Brotherhood (founded 1928), Salafism, Wahhabism — represent a specific political ideology using Islamic language. They claim to restore authentic Islam; their relationship to classical Islam is contested by Muslim scholars worldwide. The conflation of "Islam" with "Islamism" in Western media is a failure of distinction that this curriculum corrects.

### Part 10: Islam as a Worldview — The Six Questions

1. **Metaphysics:** God (Allah) is absolutely one — the creator and sustainer of everything. The universe is God's creation and subject to God's will.
2. **Epistemology:** The Quran is the ultimate authority — God's direct, uncreated word. The Hadith and scholarly consensus provide supplementary guidance.
3. **Anthropology:** Humans are God's khalifah (stewards/vicegerents) on earth — not fallen in the Christian sense but forgetful and in need of guidance. Human dignity is inherent.
4. **Ethics:** Morality comes from God's commands as revealed in the Quran and Sunnah. Right action is submission to God's will.
5. **Teleology:** Life's purpose is submission to God and preparation for the Day of Judgment. Success is paradise (Jannah); failure is hellfire (Jahannam).
6. **Authority:** God, as revealed through the Quran and the example of Muhammad. Scholarly consensus (ijma) interprets and applies divine guidance.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: Reading the Quran [Early Packet]
Read the assigned Quranic passages in translation. Discuss: How does this text feel different from the Bible? What does it claim about itself? What is the tone? What does it assume about its audience?

### Study 2: Muhammad's Life [Early-Mid Packet]
Read a biography or biographical summary. Map Muhammad's roles: prophet, statesman, judge, military commander, community organizer. Discuss: What does it mean that Muhammad combined all of these? How does this shape Islam's relationship to political authority?

### Study 3: The Five Pillars in Practice [Mid-Packet]
For each pillar, discuss not just what it is but what it produces. What kind of person does five daily prayers produce? What does mandatory charitable giving do to a community? What does fasting during Ramadan teach?

### Study 4: The Golden Age [Mid-Packet]
Study the intellectual achievements of Islamic civilization. Pick one contribution (algebra, optics, medicine, philosophy) and trace its impact on world history. Discuss: Why was Islamic civilization so intellectually productive during this period? What conditions enabled it?

### Study 5: Sunni-Shia Comparison [Mid-to-Late Packet]
Map the Sunni and Shia positions. What do they agree on? Where do they diverge? How has the split shaped history and contemporary politics?

### Study 6: Jihad — Careful Analysis [Late Packet]
Distinguish the greater and lesser jihad. Read the classical rules for the lesser jihad. Then examine a contemporary jihadist claim. Where does it follow the classical rules? Where does it violate them? What does this exercise teach about the relationship between a tradition and those who claim to act in its name?

### Study 7: Steel-Man Exercise [End of Packet]
Present Islam's strongest case as a worldview. If you are not Muslim, can you present it in terms a devout Muslim would recognize as fair and even beautiful?

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Internal Logic
**Developing well:** The student can explain tawhid and trace its implications through Islamic theology, law, and politics. They understand why separating religion from public life is incoherent from a traditional Islamic perspective.
**Developing slowly:** The student lists facts about Islam without understanding the theological logic that connects them.

### 2. Honest Complexity
**Developing well:** The student can present both the golden age and the slave trade, both the Five Pillars and the dhimmi system, without collapsing either into the other.
**Developing slowly:** Either all-positive or all-negative — cannot hold complexity.

### 3. Distinction Between Tradition and Distortion
**Developing well:** The student can explain why ISIS is not simply "what Islam teaches" while also acknowledging the textual resources that extremists exploit.
**Developing slowly:** Either "Islam has nothing to do with terrorism" (overcorrection) or "Islam is inherently violent" (stereotype).
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Fear of the Topic
Some mentors are uncomfortable teaching Islam — afraid of getting it wrong, afraid of being perceived as either too sympathetic or too critical. The cure for this is preparation. Read the sources. Know the theology. Present it honestly. The student will absorb your confidence or your anxiety.

### Treating Islam as Monolithic
Islam contains enormous internal diversity — Sunni, Shia, Sufi, Ibadi; four major Sunni legal schools; reformist, traditionalist, and modernist currents. Statements like "Islam says..." are almost always oversimplifications. Help the student see the internal diversity.
